meta.title: Roomvu — Website Redesign
meta.subtitle: A structured UX research sprint to fix content discovery, mobile experience, and location relevance for a real estate video marketing platform.
meta.company: Academic Project · Type C — UX Research Sprint

data.headline: Good content. Wrong architecture.
data.body: Roomvu produces real estate video content that agents genuinely need — market reports, property walkthroughs, mobile-friendly clips for social media. The platform isn't failing because the content is bad. It's failing because agents can't get to the content fast enough to use it. In an 8–12 hour sprint, I ran a structured heuristic audit, identified the root failure points, and mapped a redesign direction grounded in a real user persona and competitive benchmarking.

data.label: for real estate listings with video content
data.body: Source: National Association of Realtors / industry listing data. Video works. Roomvu has it. The design problem is that the agents who need it most — mobile, time-pressured, locally focused — can't access it without friction that compounds every visit.

items.title: Heuristic evaluation
items.description: Evaluated roomvu.com against Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics. Documented every friction point with severity rating (High · Medium · Low) and a specific recommendation for each.

items.title: Competitive benchmarking
items.description: Benchmarked against CREA, RE/MAX, Royal LePage, and Zillow to identify where Roomvu falls short in content structure, location filtering, and mobile responsiveness.

items.title: Persona and empathy mapping
items.description: Built a composite persona (Robert Phil, Vancouver agent) to make the abstract audit findings concrete — and to pressure-test whether each redesign direction would actually solve his specific problems.

items.title: Redesign direction mapping
items.description: Translated the top audit findings into three design direction decisions, each with a clear rationale and an honest acknowledgement of what that direction gives up.

data.title: Site Evaluation — roomvu.com

rows.finding: No video category structure on the homepage — users must scroll through an undifferentiated feed to find relevant content types.
rows.recommendation: Introduce featured category rows (Market Reports, Mobile Friendly, Listings) with clear labels above the fold.

rows.finding: Layout breaks below 768px viewport width. Primary CTAs clip off-screen and the card grid does not reflow for touch interaction.
rows.recommendation: Rebuild card grid as a responsive component. Add sticky mobile nav and ensure all touch targets meet 44px minimum height.

rows.finding: No location entry point visible above the fold. The content feed defaults to a generic national view regardless of user location.
rows.recommendation: Auto-detect or prompt for location on first visit. Persist preference and surface region-relevant content as the default view.

rows.finding: No filtering, sorting, or search controls for the video library. Discovery relies entirely on passive scrolling.
rows.recommendation: Add lightweight filter controls (location, content type, date) and a search field accessible from the library header.

rows.finding: Hero section does not explain what content types exist or why they benefit agents. There is no outcome-oriented messaging.
rows.recommendation: Rewrite hero to explicitly name content types and frame them around agent outcomes — save time, win more listings, stay current.

rows.finding: No onboarding flow or tooltips for first-time users. Platform capabilities are discovered only through unguided exploration.
rows.recommendation: Add a lightweight first-session prompt surfacing three key features: location filter, content categories, and the one-click share tool.

data.note: Evaluated against Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics. Severity — High: blocks core task completion · Medium: degrades experience quality · Low: polish opportunity.

items.headline: The problem is findability, not content quality
items.body: The audit found zero issues with Roomvu's actual content. Every high-severity finding was a structural problem — navigation, filtering, mobile layout. Agents aren't leaving because the videos are bad. They're leaving because they can't find the video they need before they run out of patience.

items.headline: Location is the missing axis across everything
items.body: Three of the six audit findings either directly or indirectly connect to missing location context. Real estate is hyper-local by nature — Vancouver agents have no use for Zillow-style Florida content. Yet the platform presents every agent the same generic national feed. Location isn't a filter. It should be the primary organising principle.

items.headline: Mobile is where the decision happens, not the desktop
items.body: Agents check content between client appointments — on their phone, in a car, with two minutes to spare. The current mobile layout breaks entirely below 768px. This isn't a responsiveness polish task. The navigation architecture itself breaks at small sizes, which means the fix is a rebuild, not a patch.

items.name: Robert Phil
items.biography: Tech-savvy real estate agent with several years of experience in Vancouver's competitive market. Uses video content regularly to differentiate listings — Roomvu-style market report clips are exactly what he needs. He checks platforms between client meetings, on his phone, and expects to find relevant content within 30 seconds or he moves on.
items.goal: Find Vancouver-specific market report and listing videos quickly enough to share with a client before the next showing — without sitting at a desktop to do it.
items.needs: Location filter that defaults to Vancouver without manual setup every session. Mobile layout that works one-handed. Category structure so he can go straight to 'Market Reports' without scrolling past Listings content.
items.frustrations: Opens Roomvu on mobile between appointments [mobile feature] — layout breaks, CTAs are off-screen, he bounces. When he does get to the video library on desktop, there's no location filter [location feature] so he scrolls through Florida and Texas content to find Vancouver. By the time he finds what he needs, he could have just searched YouTube.
items.why_use: Roomvu produces content Robert genuinely can't replicate himself — branded, market-specific clips ready to share with clients. When the mobile layout works, he opens the app between appointments, taps 'Market Reports', finds a Vancouver clip, and shares it to his client in under 90 seconds [mobile layout + category navigation]. When the location filter defaults to his city, he never scrolls past Toronto or Calgary content — every session starts where he is [location-persistent filter]. Those two moments are the only reason he returns. Without them, he's back to YouTube.

before.label: Root problems identified
items: Single undifferentiated feed — no categories, no hierarchy, no entry point for a specific content type
Location invisible — every agent sees the same generic national content regardless of their market
Mobile architecture broken — navigation structure collapses below 768px, not a responsiveness issue
Hero communicates nothing — no content types named, no agent benefit stated, no reason to stay

after.label: Design direction proposed
items: Location-first entry — prompt or auto-detect city on first visit, persist as the content filter default
Category-led homepage — distinct content rows above the fold so agents can navigate by type, not by scroll
Mobile-first rebuild — new responsive grid, sticky nav, 44px touch targets, architecture that works at 375px
Outcome-led hero — names the content types and frames them around agent wins, not platform features

items.headline: Location-first over content-first
items.body:
**A:** Global content-first, location as optional filter — lower friction for first-time visitors. **B:** Location-prompted entry — detect or ask city before surfacing content.

**Chose B** — three of six high-severity audit findings trace back to missing location context. An agent who lands on Vancouver-irrelevant content and sees no way to fix it doesn't come back. **Cost:** Location gating adds a step for new users who just want to browse without commitment.

items.headline: Category-led layout over search-led discovery
items.body:
**A:** Prominent search bar as primary navigation — familiar, works for known queries. **B:** Category-led homepage with labelled rows per content type (Market Reports · Listings · Mobile-Friendly).

**Chose B** — agents browsing for inspiration don't know what to search for; they need to see what exists. Keyword search doesn't help when you don't know the query yet. **Cost:** Categories must be actively maintained. A stale category structure is worse than no structure at all.

items.headline: Full mobile rebuild over responsive patching
items.body:
**A:** Add responsive breakpoints to the existing layout — faster, less disruptive. **B:** Rebuild the mobile navigation architecture from scratch.

**Chose B (with honest caveat)** — the existing navigation collapses at small sizes in a way media queries alone cannot fix. The layout hierarchy itself is wrong. Patching would delay the problem, not solve it. **Cost:** A full rebuild is a larger engineering commitment than this sprint could prototype end-to-end. This direction was proposed, not shipped.

data.headline: What the proposed homepage looks like in action.
data.body: This prototype demonstrates the three core direction decisions: location-first entry, category-led navigation, and location-aware content titles. Change the city to see how the content adapts. Switch category tabs to see the content hierarchy that was missing from the original site.

data.text: Location was missing from every layer — not as a feature, but as the fundamental organising logic. The right content in the wrong context is just noise. Every agent's world is six square miles. The platform was built for a country.
